duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not check newInstance != oldInstance, allowing an SGX enclave to reset its validSince timestamp indefinitely by self-replacing, bypassing the 180-day INSTANCE_EXPIRY and key rotation requirement.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All five describe the identical code defect in _replaceInstance (no newInstance != oldInstance check). ECON-H001 is selected as representative for its CRITICAL severity rating and comprehensive attack economics including indefinite attack duration and full L2 state manipulation potential.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true after key rotation, allowing the newInstance address to be re-registered via registerInstance() to obtain a second instance ID, breaking the one-address-one-instance invariant.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All seven describe the same missing addressRegistered state update in _replaceInstance. OPS-H001 is selected for its CRITICAL severity rating and precise identification of the code gap between _addInstances (which checks addressRegistered) and _replaceInstance (which never sets it). Note: TECH2-H003 refers to the first entry with that ID titled 'Missing state update in _replaceInstance'.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: GuardianProver.approve() computes the approval hash as keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) excluding _proof, allowing the threshold-triggering guardian to unilaterally control proof.data (including the RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND magic value) forwarded to TaikoL1.proveBlock().
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All ten (including both entries with ID TECH2-H007) describe the same fundamental flaw: the guardian approval hash excludes _proof, granting the last signer unilateral control over proof.data. TECH-H014 adds a speculative replay concern but shares the same root cause. ECON-H003 is selected for its clear economic impact analysis (250 TKO per block extraction) and precise precondition enumeration.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed() uses safeTransferFrom(_meta.coinbase, _blk.assignedProver, proverFee) where coinbase is proposer-controlled via params.coinbase and not included in the prover's signed assignment hash, enabling arbitrary ERC20 theft from any address that has approved AssignmentHook.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All five describe the identical confused-deputy vulnerability where an attacker sets params.coinbase to a victim's address to steal their approved ERC20 tokens. ECON-H011 is selected for its CRITICAL severity, comprehensive attack economics (infinite ROI since capital is recovered), and detailed victim impact analysis covering the full allowance at risk.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Block minTier is derived from keccak256(block.prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number), which is fully deterministic from observable L1 state, allowing proposers to compute the required tier before submitting and selectively skip blocks that require expensive tiers (e.g., SGX_ZKVM).
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All eleven describe the same predictable randomness issue in tier selection. ECON-H004 is selected for its HIGH severity, precise cost analysis (0 capital, 100% cost avoidance), and clear articulation of the security model degradation. Note: TECH2-H003 here refers to the second entry with that ID titled 'Permissionless proposer can bypass high-security tiers'.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Assigned prover is permanently locked out from submitting higher-tier proofs (L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED) after their initial proof is contested, causing them to lose livenessBond/2 during verification when a third party resolves the dispute, even if the original proof was correct.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All six describe the same game-theoretic flaw where _checkProverPermission blocks the assigned prover from defending their correct proof after contest. ECON-H005 is selected for its detailed griefing economics (3:1 damage ratio, 125 TKO loss per block) and clear identification of the verification penalty path. Note: TECH2-H004 refers to the first entry with that ID titled 'Assigned prover is permanently locked out'.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: TaikoL1.proveBlock is gated by whenProvingNotPaused with no exemption for guardian-tier proofs, preventing the highest-authority emergency override mechanism from functioning when proving is paused.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All four describe the identical architectural flaw where guardian emergency proofs are blocked by the same proving pause that gates regular provers. TECH-H005 is selected for its HIGH severity, clear identification of the deadlock scenario, and precise code reference to the modifier chain.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: The proving window calculation uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt), and repeated pause/unpause toggling by chain_pauser resets lastUnpausedAt, indefinitely extending the assigned prover's exclusive window and preventing other provers from submitting.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same vulnerability in _checkProverPermission where lastUnpausedAt manipulation extends the proving window. TECH-H007 is selected for its more detailed code analysis covering both pauseProving(false) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset paths.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: A malicious prover can DoS proposers by deploying a smart contract that reverts on ETH receipt, causing the AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed ETH payment via sendEther to revert the entire proposeBlock transaction.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All three describe the same griefing vector via reverting prover fallback function in the ETH fee payment path. ECON2-H005 is selected for its economic analysis covering MEV collusion angle and asymmetric cost profile. Note: TECH2-H005 here refers to the first entry with that ID titled 'Malicious prover can DoS proposers'.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Systematic contestation of valid proofs stalls sequential block verification by setting ts.contester, forcing higher-tier resolution and cooldown delays, effectively creating a repeatable finality delay attack.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same attack of systematically contesting proofs to delay verification. ECON-H010 is selected for its comprehensive cost analysis (1500 TKO per block, 25+ hour delay) and multi-tier escalation walkthrough.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: LibDepositing.processDeposits appends a fee deposit to the ETH ring buffer and increments numEthDeposits without checking canDepositEthToL2 capacity, potentially blocking user deposits when the buffer is near capacity.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same capacity check bypass in processDeposits for fee deposits. TECH-H016 is selected for its more detailed analysis of the ring buffer wraparound and collision mechanics.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: ETH deposit ring buffer can be filled by an attacker spamming minimum deposits, blocking subsequent user deposits until proposers process enough to clear capacity.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same bounded-queue DoS attack on the ETH deposit system. OPS-H013 is selected for its HIGH severity and more detailed operational analysis including attacker cost modeling.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: LibDepositing.depositEtherToL2 sends ETH to the bridge contract via external call before recording the deposit in the ring buffer, violating checks-effects-interactions and creating potential desynchronization if the bridge has unexpected behavior.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same ordering violation where external ETH transfer to bridge precedes local state update. TECH2-H005 is selected for its HIGH severity and more detailed analysis of bridge dependency risks. Note: TECH2-H005 here refers to the second entry with that ID titled 'Deposit queue integrity risk'.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: The _authorizePause override pattern using modifier-only empty body in TaikoL1 and other EssentialContract inheritors may not correctly enforce access control, potentially making pause/unpause and pauseProving callable by unauthorized accounts.
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both describe the same root cause: the modifier-only _authorizePause override pattern may not properly enforce access control. TECH-H012 is selected for its CRITICAL severity and specific focus on pauseProving which has the highest impact (stalling the entire proving/verification pipeline).
